pi_items.source: NACA airfoil designation standard; verified via airfoiltools.com and NASA/CFD documentation. NACA 0012 is a symmetric airfoil with 12% maximum thickness.

Verified via NASA GRC and aerodynamics literature. Mach 0.0=incompressible baseline; Mach 0.3=threshold where compressibility effects become significant (>5% density variation); Mach 0.5=low subsonic regime (0.1-0.6). These are standard values for compressibility studies.

Verified via Cadence analysis and aerospace literature. Reynolds number 3,000,000 is a typical value for full-scale aircraft and large wind turbine rotors (megawatt-class turbines operate at 2-8 million Re). Falls in high Reynolds number regime where flow is predominantly turbulent.

Task description specifies NCrit=9. Verified via XFOIL documentation and CFD forums. Value of 9 corresponds to average wind tunnel conditions (0.07% freestream turbulence) and is a standard reference value for XFoil boundary layer transition modeling.

Task-defined minimum. Task specifies angle of attack range -5 to 15 degrees with 0.5 degree step = 41 computed points, exceeding minimum of 20. XFoil typically requires 50+ points; 20 is conservative minimum threshold.
Task description explicitly specifies range and step. This yields 41 data points per polar, well above XFoil standards.
Task description requires THREE separate polar analyses, one for each Mach value (0.0, 0.3, 0.5).

privileged_info_summary: The mach_compressibility_study task uses NACA 0012, a real symmetric airfoil with 12% maximum thickness and extensive documented aerodynamic data. The three Mach numbers (0.0, 0.3, 0.5) are verified standard values for compressibility studies: Mach 0.0 is incompressible baseline, Mach 0.3 marks the threshold where compressibility effects become significant, and Mach 0.5 is in the low subsonic regime. Reynolds number 3,000,000 is typical for full-scale aircraft and megawatt-class wind turbines. The NCrit=9 parameter represents average wind tunnel conditions (0.07% turbulence). The angle of attack range (-5 to 15 degrees, 0.5 degree step) produces 41 data points per polar, exceeding XFoil standards (>50 typical, >20 minimum). This is a synthetic task where aerodynamic polars are generated via QBlade/XFoil simulation.
